ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bread food product class; no specific class for white bread with peanut butter and jam exists. Closest defensible parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and similar products; no specific composite code for white bread with peanut butter and jam. Closest defensible category used.

serving.description: 2 slices of white bread with peanut butter and jam
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 100 g, equivalent to two slices of white bread spread with peanut butter and jam.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with peanut butter and jam — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for white bread with peanut butter and jam (cr_id F05020131). All per-100g values preserved from the source record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — white bread reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: White bread has a well-established GI of approximately 70 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr). The composite dish (white bread + peanut butter + jam) is estimated at GI 70 because white bread dominates the carbohydrate load; peanut butter fat and protein provide modest GI attenuation but jam sugar partially offsets this. Net GI estimated at 70 (high). GL per 100 g serving = (70 × 45.02) / 100 = 31.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium (377 mg/100 g) combined with low potassium (93 mg/100 g) and high added sugar from jam (≈10 g/100 g) with insufficient dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this food due to its high sodium content (377 mg/100 g), very low potassium-to-sodium ratio, and added sugar from jam that exceeds the hypertension sugar threshold with inadequate fibre offset; substituting wholegrain bread, no-added-sugar spread, and a low-sodium peanut butter would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~70) driven by refined white bread, combined with concentrated added sugar from jam (≈10 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g), producing a high glycaemic load of 31.5 per 100 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food because the high-GI white bread combined with jam-derived added sugar creates a high glycaemic load (GL ≈ 31.5/100 g) that will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; switching to wholegrain bread, reducing jam quantity, and using natural peanut butter without added sugar are the minimum modifications required.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (9.08 g/100 g) with saturated fat present, high sodium (377 mg/100 g), and high added sugar from jam contributing to atherogenic and hypertensive risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this food poses moderate concern due to its elevated sodium (377 mg/100 g), added sugar from jam, and saturated fat content; portion control is essential and substitution with wholegrain bread, unsalted natural peanut butter, and a no-added-sugar fruit spread is strongly recommended to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar from jam (≈10 g/100 g) and high-GI refined white bread promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food as the combination of refined white bread and jam-derived sugar promotes systemic inflammation; replacing white bread with wholegrain and jam with a polyphenol-rich spread such as berry compote without added sugar would reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with peanut butter and jam is a widely consumed composite bread-based dish consisting of sliced white bread spread with peanut butter and a fruit jam or jelly. It is a staple breakfast and snack item popular across Western countries and widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The white bread provides a soft, mild-flavoured base with a refined carbohydrate profile (45 g/100 g), while peanut butter contributes protein (10.7 g/100 g), fat (9.08 g/100 g), and a creamy, nutty flavour. Jam adds sweetness and concentrated fruit sugar (≈10 g/100 g total sugar). The dish requires no cooking — it is assembled by spreading peanut butter and jam onto sliced bread. Nutritionally, the food is energy-dense at 301 kcal/100 g with a high glycaemic index (~70) driven by the refined white bread and jam sugar, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. Sodium is elevated at 377 mg/100 g, primarily from the bread, while potassium is low at 93 mg/100 g, creating an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio for blood pressure management. Dietary fibre is modest at 2.8 g/100 g, insufficient to meaningfully attenuate the glycaemic response. Iron content is notable at 4.22 mg/100 g and calcium at 158 mg/100 g, likely from fortified white bread. The food is plant-based, dairy-free, and suitable for vegetarians and vegans, but contains gluten and peanut allergens. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack and is known by the abbreviation PB&J in North American contexts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020131) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is estimated from the well-established white bread GI reference (University of Sydney GI Database) with documented analogous estimation basis. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for peanut butter and white bread as HPB did not report saturated fat breakdown.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05020131 as of 2026. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for white bread and peanut butter components as a reasonable approximation; this is the only field not directly from HPB. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g as reported by HPB — this is a plant-based composite food containing no animal-derived fat sources (peanut butter and plant-based jam); confirmed zero per HPB source. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI reference from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite GI may be marginally lower due to fat and protein from peanut butter, but white bread dominates the carbohydrate load and the estimate is conservatively retained at 70. Dietary tags include plant_based as per input record; halal status assumed based on plant-based ingredients but individual product halal certification should be verified. Kosher status not confirmed.
